user_goals_summary: Writes conversion-focused landing page copy that builds forward momentum through tension and quiet confidence rather than aggressive selling. Values seamlessness — every element (headlines, body, CTAs, social proof) should feel like one continuous persuasive thread. Prefers compressed, rhythmic prose that lets readers feel benefits in their bodies rather than understand them intellectually.

rubric.name: Narrative Tension & Forward Pull
rubric.description: Headlines and section openings create an itch — a small knot of tension the reader needs resolved. Copy compels forward rather than summarizing. The reader should feel 'I need to read the next line,' not 'I'm informed.'

dimensions.label: Headline creates an unresolved tension, question, or gap that demands the reader continue
Key claims are not front-loaded in a way that removes the reason to keep reading
Subheadline or first line of body copy begins resolving the tension the headline created
Each new section or block opens with language that earns the reader's continued attention

rubric.name: Seamless Persuasive Thread
rubric.description: Every element — body copy, CTAs, subheads, social proof — feels like it belongs to the same argument and the same voice. No seams, no register shifts, no moments where it feels like 'the copy handed off to a different department.' CTAs grow organically from the preceding logic so clicking feels inevitable.

dimensions.label: CTA language grows directly from the idea or emotional beat that precedes it
Tone and voice remain consistent across all copy elements (headlines, body, CTAs, microcopy)
The action the reader is asked to take feels like the only logical conclusion of the argument
Core themes introduced early are revisited and reinforced later, creating bookend structure

rubric.name: Quiet Confidence in Tone
rubric.description: Tone is conversational, knowing, and slightly unsettling — not adversarial or preachy. The reader should feel like they're finally 'in on something,' not like they're being lectured or sold to. Confidence comes from understated certainty, not volume.

dimensions.label: Copy makes the reader feel like they're being let in on something rather than being targeted
Competitor or industry critiques are implied or quietly provoked, not finger-pointing or aggressive
Language feels like a smart friend explaining something, not a brand making a pitch
Confident claims are grounded in specific evidence or logic, not empty bravado

rubric.name: Benefits as Felt Experience
rubric.description: Benefits are described in terms of what the reader will physically or emotionally feel — not as abstract features or technical specs. The mechanism can appear, but it serves the felt outcome. Readers should be able to imagine the experience in their body.

dimensions.label: Benefit descriptions use sensory or experiential language the reader can feel
Technical or scientific details appear only to make the felt benefit more credible, not as ends in themselves
Benefits are anchored to specific recognizable moments in the reader's life (e.g., 3am wake-ups, morning fog)
Each benefit implicitly or explicitly contrasts with a worse experience the reader already knows

rubric.name: Integrated Social Proof
rubric.description: Customer voices are woven into the body of the argument, not separated into testimonial blocks. They appear at the exact moment they reinforce the point being made, feel like a friend texting — not a quote module — and are never formally introduced.

dimensions.label: Customer quotes appear mid-benefit or mid-section, not in a separate testimonials area
Quotes are not introduced with phrasing like 'here's what our customers say' — they simply appear
Customer quotes include at least one concrete, specific detail that generic praise would lack
Each quote directly reinforces or provides evidence for the brand claim immediately surrounding it

rubric.name: Rhythmic Compression
rubric.description: Key moments use short, punchy fragments for impact. Sentences vary in length to create rhythm, and the most important beats are often the shortest. Closers land through compression — stripping a line down to its essential force.

dimensions.label: Sections or benefit blocks end with short, compressed lines that land with disproportionate impact
Sentence length varies deliberately — longer explanatory lines are followed by short punches
Copy does not use more words than necessary; filler phrases and hedging are absent
The most important lines in the copy are among the shortest

coaching_notes: This user has an exceptionally precise editorial instinct — they can diagnose exactly why something isn't working and articulate the feeling they want, but they need options to react to rather than generating from scratch. Never present a single solution; offer 3-5 directions with a recommended pick. Their non-negotiable is forward momentum: if a headline, CTA, or section opening doesn't compel the reader into the next line, it's a miss regardless of how accurate or well-written it is. They strongly prefer 'quiet confidence' over aggression — when in doubt, choose the more understated option.

rubric.name: Seamless Persuasive Thread
rubric.description: Every element — body copy, CTAs, subheads, social proof — feels like it belongs to the same argument and the same voice. No seams, no register shifts, no moments where it feels like 'the copy handed off to a different department.' CTAs grow organically from the preceding logic so clicking feels inevitable.

dimensions.label: CTA language grows directly from the idea or emotional beat that precedes it
Tone and voice remain consistent across all copy elements (headlines, body, CTAs, microcopy)
The action the reader is asked to take feels like the only logical conclusion of the argument
Core themes introduced early are revisited and reinforced later, creating bookend structure

They strongly prefer 'quiet confidence' over aggression — when in doubt, choose the more understated option.

rubric.name: Narrative Tension & Forward Pull
rubric.description: Headlines and section openings create an itch — a small knot of tension the reader needs resolved. A headline that summarizes the product is a missed opportunity. The best headlines make you need to read the next line. Copy compels forward rather than summarizing. Tension can be implicit — a bold claim, a contrarian framing, an observation that reframes the reader's experience — not just an explicit 'what if' question. Avoid manufacturing tension through obvious rhetorical devices.

dimensions.label: Headline creates forward momentum through contrarian framing, an unresolved implication, or a gap — not necessarily a direct question, and never by flagging its own intent
Key claims are not front-loaded in a way that removes the reason to keep reading
Subheadline or first line of body copy begins resolving the tension the headline created
Each new section or block opens with language that earns the reader's continued attention
No headline simply describes or summarizes the product — every headline creates a reason to keep reading

rubric.name: Seamless Persuasive Thread
rubric.description: Every element — body copy, CTAs, subheads, social proof — feels like it belongs to the same argument and the same voice. No seams, no register shifts, no moments where it feels like 'the copy handed off to a different department.' CTAs grow organically from the preceding logic so clicking feels inevitable. NOT clicking should feel like leaving something unfinished.

dimensions.label: CTA language grows directly from the idea or emotional beat that precedes it
Tone and voice remain consistent across all copy elements (headlines, body, CTAs, microcopy)
The action the reader is asked to take feels like the only logical conclusion of the argument
Core themes introduced early are revisited and reinforced later, creating bookend structure
There is exactly one primary CTA — no secondary actions compete for attention or dilute the main conversion goal

rubric.name: Benefits Over Features — Always
rubric.description: Features tell you what something does. Benefits tell you why you care. Copy should spend the majority of its time on benefits. Every feature mention must be immediately followed by a 'so what?' — the translation into something the reader actually feels or gains. The 'so what?' translation should land with ease — a simple, grounded articulation of the felt benefit. Avoid clever or elaborate metaphors that call attention to themselves. If the phrasing sounds like it was written to impress, it's too much. A list of features without that translation reads like a spec sheet, not a landing page.

dimensions.label: Benefit descriptions use sensory or experiential language the reader can feel
Technical or scientific details appear only to make the felt benefit more credible, not as ends in themselves
Benefits are anchored to specific recognizable moments in the reader's life (e.g., 3am wake-ups, morning fog)
Each benefit implicitly or explicitly contrasts with a worse experience the reader already knows
Every feature mentioned is immediately followed by its 'so what?' — the benefit it produces for the reader
Copy time is weighted toward benefits — features are present but never dominate the page

rubric.name: Integrated Social Proof
rubric.description: Customer voices are woven into the body of the argument, not separated into testimonial blocks. A customer quote mid-paragraph that reinforces the point you're making is stronger than a testimonials section at the bottom. Quotes appear at the exact moment they reinforce the point being made, feel like a friend texting — not a quote module — and are never formally introduced.

dimensions.label: Customer quotes appear mid-benefit or mid-section, not in a separate testimonials area
Quotes are not introduced with phrasing like 'here's what our customers say' — they simply appear
Customer quotes include at least one concrete, specific detail that generic praise would lack
Each quote directly reinforces or provides evidence for the brand claim immediately surrounding it

rubric.name: Quiet Confidence in Tone
rubric.description: Tone is conversational, knowing, and confident — not adversarial, preachy, or hype-driven. The reader should feel like they're talking to a smart friend who knows their stuff, not being pitched to or talked down to. Confidence comes from understated certainty and specific evidence, not volume or buzzwords. No hype words without substance behind them.

dimensions.label: Copy makes the reader feel like they're being let in on something rather than being targeted
Competitor or industry critiques are implied or quietly provoked, not finger-pointing or aggressive
Language feels like a smart friend explaining something, not a brand making a pitch
Confident claims are grounded in specific evidence or logic, not empty bravado
No hype words (revolutionary, game-changing, best-in-class, etc.) appear without concrete substance immediately backing them up
Copy never talks down to the reader — assumes they are smart, capable of making their own decision
Benefit language is grounded and direct — avoids extended metaphors or figurative constructions that feel like they're straining for effect rather than communicating naturally

rubric.name: Category Distinctiveness
rubric.description: The copy should not sound like every other brand in the category. If you could swap the brand name out and drop this copy onto a competitor's page without anyone noticing, it has failed. Distinctive voice, specific proof points, and an original angle on the customer's problem are non-negotiable.

dimensions.label: Copy could not be mistaken for a competitor's — voice, angle, and specific claims are brand-distinctive
The customer's problem is framed in a way that feels fresh and specific, not generic category language
No clichés or stock phrases that appear routinely across the category (e.g., 'designed for people like you,' 'finally, a product that...')

user_goals_summary: Writes conversion-focused landing page copy that builds forward momentum through tension and quiet confidence rather than aggressive selling. Values seamlessness — every element (headlines, body, CTAs, social proof) should feel like one continuous persuasive thread. Prefers compressed, rhythmic prose that lets readers feel benefits in their bodies rather than understand them intellectually.
coaching_notes: This user has an exceptionally precise editorial instinct — they can diagnose exactly why something isn't working and articulate the feeling they want, but they need options to react to rather than generating from scratch. Never present a single solution; offer 3-5 directions with a recommended pick. Their non-negotiable is forward momentum: if a headline, CTA, or section opening doesn't compel the reader into the next line, it's a miss regardless of how accurate or well-written it is. They strongly prefer 'quiet confidence' over aggression — when in doubt, choose the more understated option.

user_goals_summary: Copy should feel like a single seamless argument that pulls the reader forward through subtle tension, grounds claims in concrete substance, and weaves social proof directly into the persuasive flow as evidence — not decoration. The voice is conversational and quietly confident, landing with ease rather than performance. Every element from headline to CTA belongs to the same continuous thought.

rubric.name: Narrative Tension & Forward Pull
rubric.description: Headlines and section openings create an itch — a small knot of tension the reader needs resolved. A headline that summarizes the product is a missed opportunity. Tension should be implicit — a provocation, a reframe, an unresolved implication — never a device that explains itself or announces its own cleverness. If the tension feels 'on-the-nose,' it's not tension, it's a label.

dimensions.label: Headline creates forward momentum through an unresolved implication, contrarian framing, or tension gap — never by simply describing or summarizing the product
Tension devices are subtle and understated — no rhetorical questions or provocations that explain their own intent or feel 'on-the-nose'
Key claims are not front-loaded in a way that removes the reason to keep reading
Subheadline or first line of body copy begins resolving the tension the headline created
Each new section or block opens with language that earns the reader's continued attention

rubric.name: Seamless Persuasive Thread
rubric.description: Every element — body copy, CTAs, subheads, social proof — feels like it belongs to the same argument and the same voice. No seams, no register shifts, no moments where it feels like 'the copy handed off to a different department.' CTAs grow organically from the preceding emotional beat so clicking feels like the natural exhale. NOT clicking should feel like leaving something unfinished.

dimensions.label: CTA language grows directly from the idea or emotional beat that precedes it — clicking feels like the natural next breath, not a handoff to a sales page
Tone and voice remain consistent across all copy elements (headlines, body, CTAs, microcopy)
The action the reader is asked to take feels like the only logical conclusion of the argument
Core themes introduced early are revisited and reinforced later, creating bookend structure
There is exactly one primary CTA — no secondary actions compete for attention or dilute the main conversion goal

rubric.name: Integrated Social Proof
rubric.description: Customer voices are woven into the body of the argument, not separated into testimonial blocks. Critically, quotes should complete open thoughts — the copy builds toward a moment and hands off to the customer voice to finish it. A quote that echoes an already-resolved idea is a footnote; a quote that resolves an open one is evidence. They should feel like a friend texting, not a testimonials module that snuck in.

dimensions.label: Customer quotes appear mid-benefit or mid-section, not in a separate testimonials area
The paragraph preceding the quote builds toward but does NOT fully resolve the idea — the quote is what completes or closes the thought
The copy immediately before the quote lands on a specific felt moment that makes the quote feel like confirmation, not decoration
Quotes are not introduced with phrasing like 'here's what our customers say' — they simply appear in the flow
Customer quotes include at least one concrete, specific detail that generic praise would lack

rubric.name: Benefits Grounded in Substance
rubric.description: Copy spends the majority of its time on benefits, not features. But benefits must be grounded in something real — a plainly stated mechanism, a specific decision the brand made, or a concrete change the reader can picture. Evocative benefit language that never explains what actually changes reads as evasive. Every feature needs a 'so what?' translation, and every benefit needs at least a sentence of substance anchoring it.

dimensions.label: Benefit descriptions use sensory or experiential language the reader can feel in their body or daily life
At least one plainly stated sentence explains what the product actually does or what decision the brand made — not in metaphor, just directly
Benefits are anchored to specific recognizable moments in the reader's life (e.g., 3am wake-ups, the anxious mirror check, morning fog)
Each benefit implicitly or explicitly contrasts with a worse experience the reader already knows
Every feature or technical detail mentioned is immediately followed by its 'so what?' — the benefit it produces for the reader

rubric.name: Quiet Confidence in Tone
rubric.description: Tone is conversational, knowing, and confident — not adversarial, preachy, or hype-driven. The reader should feel like they're talking to a smart friend who knows their stuff, not being pitched to or talked down to. Confidence comes from understated certainty and specific evidence, not volume or buzzwords. Language should land with ease — if a line sounds like it was written to impress, it's trying too hard.

dimensions.label: Copy makes the reader feel like they're being let in on something rather than being targeted
Competitor or industry critiques are implied or quietly provoked, not finger-pointing or aggressive
Language feels like a smart friend explaining something, not a brand making a pitch
Lines land with ease — no extended metaphors, overwrought constructions, or phrasing that sounds clever in a brief but reads as 'trying too hard' in actual copy
No hype words (revolutionary, game-changing, best-in-class, etc.) appear without concrete substance immediately backing them up

rubric.name: Category Distinctiveness
rubric.description: The copy should not sound like every other brand in the category. If you could swap the brand name out and drop this copy onto a competitor's page without anyone noticing, it has failed. Distinctive voice, specific proof points, and an original angle on the customer's problem are non-negotiable.

dimensions.label: Copy could not be mistaken for a competitor's — voice, angle, and specific claims are brand-distinctive
The customer's problem is framed in a way that feels fresh and specific, not generic category language
No clichés or stock phrases that appear routinely across the category (e.g., 'designed for people like you,' 'finally, a product that...')

coaching_notes: Non-negotiables: (1) Headlines must create tension, never summarize — but tension must be subtle and implicit; if the device 'explains itself,' it's failed. (2) Customer quotes must complete open thoughts mid-copy, never echo resolved ideas or live in standalone blocks — this user will iterate relentlessly until the seam disappears. (3) Benefits need at least one moment of plainly stated substance; evocative language without a grounding mechanism reads as evasive. Key trade-off: this user always chooses ease over cleverness — a natural-sounding line that communicates simply beats an elaborate construction that calls attention to itself.

If the tension feels 'on-the-nose,' it's not tension, it's a label.

dimensions.label: Copy could not be mistaken for a competitor's — voice, angle, and specific claims are brand-distinctive
The customer's problem is framed in a way that feels fresh and specific, not generic category language
No clichés or stock phrases that appear routinely across the category (e.g., 'designed for people like you,' 'finally, a product that...')

coaching_notes: This user has an exceptionally refined sense of seams — they can feel the exact moment copy shifts from one intention to another, whether it's a CTA that breaks register, a quote that floats decoratively, or a benefit that stays atmospheric without grounding itself. Fix seams before anything else. They reject tension devices that announce themselves ('on-the-nose') and clever lines that perform rather than land — subtlety and ease are non-negotiable. When they say something is 'close,' the remaining issue is almost always about integration: the right idea in the wrong structural position.

rubric.name: Narrative Tension & Forward Pull
rubric.description: Headlines and section openings create an itch — a small knot of tension the reader needs resolved. A headline that summarizes the product is a missed opportunity. Tension should be implicit — a provocation, a reframe, an unresolved implication — never a device that explains itself or announces its own cleverness. If the tension feels 'on-the-nose,' it's not tension, it's a label.

dimensions.label: Headline creates forward momentum through an unresolved implication, contrarian framing, or tension gap — never by simply describing or summarizing the product
Tension devices are subtle and earned — no rhetorical questions or provocations that explain their own intent or feel 'on-the-nose'
Key claims are not front-loaded in a way that removes the reason to keep reading
Subheadline or first line of body copy begins resolving the tension the headline created
Each new section or block opens with language that earns the reader's continued attention

rubric.name: Category Distinctiveness
rubric.description: The copy should not sound like every other brand in the category. If you could swap the brand name out and drop this copy onto a competitor's page without anyone noticing, it has failed. Distinctive voice, specific proof points, and an original angle on the customer's problem are non-negotiable.

dimensions.label: Copy could not be mistaken for a competitor's — voice, angle, and specific claims are brand-distinctive
The customer's problem is framed in a way that feels fresh and specific, not generic category language
No clichés or stock phrases that appear routinely across the category (e.g., 'designed for people like you,' 'finally, a product that...')

rubric.name: Seamless Persuasive Thread
rubric.description: Every element — body copy, CTAs, subheads, social proof — feels like it belongs to the same argument and the same voice. No seams, no register shifts, no moments where it feels like 'the copy handed off to a different department.' CTAs grow organically from the preceding emotional beat so clicking feels like the natural exhale — and the momentum leading into the CTA never deflates or resolves before the reader is asked to act.

dimensions.label: CTA language grows directly from the idea or emotional beat that precedes it — clicking feels like the natural next breath, not a handoff to a sales page
Tone and voice remain consistent across all copy elements (headlines, body, CTAs, microcopy)
The lines immediately before the CTA maintain forward energy — they do not resolve the emotional arc, tell the reader they're 'done,' or exhale before asking them to act
Core themes introduced early are revisited and reinforced later, creating bookend structure
There is exactly one primary CTA — no secondary actions compete for attention or dilute the main conversion goal

rubric.name: Integrated Social Proof
rubric.description: Customer voices are woven into the body of the argument, not separated into testimonial blocks. Critically, quotes should complete open thoughts — the copy builds toward a moment and hands off to the customer voice to finish it. A quote that echoes an already-resolved idea is a footnote; a quote that resolves an open one is evidence. At their best, customer voices become part of the narrative itself — not a formatted block quote, but a person stepping into the story.

dimensions.label: The paragraph preceding the quote builds toward but does NOT fully resolve the idea — the quote is what completes or closes the thought
The copy immediately before the quote lands on a specific felt moment that makes the quote feel like natural confirmation, not decoration
Customer quotes appear within the body of the argument — not in a separate testimonials section, not tacked onto the end of a block
Quotes are not introduced with phrasing like 'here's what our customers say' — they simply appear in the flow
Customer quotes include at least one concrete, specific detail that generic praise would lack

rubric.name: Benefits Grounded in Substance
rubric.description: Copy spends the majority of its time on benefits, not features. But benefits must be grounded in something real — a plainly stated mechanism, a specific decision the brand made, or a concrete change the reader can picture. Evocative benefit language that never explains what actually changes reads as evasive. Every feature needs a 'so what?' translation, and every benefit needs at least a sentence of substance anchoring it.

dimensions.label: Benefit descriptions use sensory or experiential language the reader can feel in their body or daily life
At least one plainly stated sentence explains what the product actually does or what decision the brand made — not in metaphor, just directly
Benefits are anchored to specific recognizable moments in the reader's life (e.g., 3am wake-ups, the anxious mirror check, morning fog)
Each benefit implicitly or explicitly contrasts with a worse experience the reader already knows
Every feature or technical detail mentioned is immediately followed by its 'so what?' — the benefit it produces for the reader

rubric.name: Quiet Confidence in Tone
rubric.description: Tone is conversational, knowing, and confident — not adversarial, preachy, or hype-driven. The reader should feel like they're talking to a smart friend who knows their stuff, not being pitched to or talked down to. Confidence comes from understated certainty and specific evidence, not volume or buzzwords. Lines should land with ease — if a line sounds like it was written to impress, it's trying too hard.

dimensions.label: Copy makes the reader feel like they're being let in on something rather than being targeted
Competitor or industry critiques are implied or quietly provoked, not finger-pointing or aggressive
Language feels like a smart friend explaining something, not a brand making a pitch
Lines land with ease — no extended metaphors, overwrought constructions, or phrasing that sounds clever in a brief but reads as 'trying too hard' in actual copy
No hype words (revolutionary, game-changing, best-in-class, etc.) appear without concrete substance immediately backing them up

user_goals_summary: Writes conversion-focused landing page copy that feels like one continuous, confident thought rather than assembled marketing modules. Values copy where every element — headlines, benefits, social proof, CTAs — earns its placement by growing from the element before it. Prioritizes subtle tension, grounded substance, and effortless tone over cleverness or hype.

coaching_notes: Non-negotiable: customer quotes must complete open thoughts, never echo resolved ones — this is the single most consistent revision trigger across all tasks. This user treats CTAs as part of the argument, not as UI elements; any CTA that feels like a handoff to 'a different department' will be flagged immediately. When in doubt between clever and clear, choose clear — this user consistently rejects overwrought phrasing ('trying too hard') and rewards pithy lines that land with ease ('No sedation. Just quiet.' and 'you bought the intention, not the habit' were standout favorites).
